narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after pouring your heart into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 44.9% chance of not passing. Picture your story through the model's eyes: it started with your Year 3 grades placing you in the 2nd decile at school, which stood out as the biggest red flag, whispering that the rigors of upper-level law might prove an uphill battle. That low LSAT score of 31 built on it, signaling to the model that the kind of analytical firepower needed to conquer the bar might be a stretch from the outset. Your Year 1 decile of 1st didn't help either, painting a picture of early struggles that echoed through your academic journey. Coming from a middle-income family added another layer, as the model associates that with fewer resources to buffer the intense demands of bar prep. Your white background and female gender, while not defining you, nudged the odds toward failure in patterns the model has seen before. Even your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.0 and full-time commitment couldn't fully counterbalance these, as they ranked lower in swaying the prediction. It's like the model saw a mosaic where these pieces—your deciles, LSAT, and background—fit together into a higher-risk profile for that final exam hurdle. This isn't about your potential or passion; it's a data-driven snapshot based on thousands like yours. The good news? You're targeting law school with intention, and with targeted boosts—like LSAT retakes or grade strategies—you could rewrite this story. Keep pushing; many have turned similar predictions around. We're rooting for you.
